message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The COVID 19 pandemic was highlighted by a rise in hospital admissions secondary to respiratory decompensation. This was accompanied by an increase in ICU admissions, endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation. As a consequence, tracheostomies became essential in preventing complications of prolonged intubation and to facilitate weaning from sedation and mechanical ventilation. With the lack of international consensus on tracheostomy technique and optimal timing, we present our experience with 377 percutaneous tracheostomies performed on critically ill COVID 19 patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To report the outcomes of critically ill patients with COVID-19 who underwent percutaneous tracheostomy during a period of 24 months.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A retrospective single-center electronic chart review was performed on all ICU patients who underwent percutaneous tracheostomy after respiratory failure secondary to COVID-19 between March 2020 to March 2022.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A total of 377 percutaneous tracheostomies were performed. The mean duration between intubation and percutaneous tracheostomy was 17.4 days (3-61). The study included 222 males (59%) and 155 females (41%).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>The mean age of patients was 56.2 years (17-94), with a mean BMI was 31.3 (14-68).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>The commonest comorbidities among patients were diabetes mellitus (50%) and hypertension (48%).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Complications were encountered in 85 cases (23%), with the commonest overall complication being minor bleeding.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>203 patients (54%) were weaned from sedation. The mean duration between tracheostomy and weaning from sedation was 7.5 days (1 – 47 days). 156 patients (41%) were weaned from MV. The mean duration between tracheostomy and weaning from MV was 12.9 days (1 – 58 days). There was a total of 236 (63%) deaths reported during the period of this study.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>No deaths were attributable to the surgical procedure.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Percutaneous tracheostomy can be safely performed in patients with COVID-19. With lack of conclusive objective data regarding the optimal timing for tracheostomy, we recommend that tracheostomy be performed as soon as possible after the 7
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    day endotracheal intubation.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Outcomes After Percutaneous Tracheostomy in Patients with COVID-19: A Single-Center Series of 377 Cases
